summary: Yegge predicts the imminent arrival of the 'Next Big Language' (NBL) and outlines the characteristics it must have to achieve mass adoption. He argues that language success requires corporate backing, C-like syntax (despite its flaws), dynamic typing with optional static types, strong performance, excellent tooling, and a comprehensive feature set. The post acknowledges that these requirements optimize for popularity rather than elegance, noting that the bar for 'not sucking' has become remarkably high. Yegge claims to know what NBL is but refuses to name it, instead providing enough hints for readers to deduce it themselves.
key_points: Most new programming languages fail because they lack corporate backing and don't meet the practical requirements for mass adoption
C-like syntax is mandatory for popularity despite being overly complicated and making code-processing tools difficult to write
Dynamic typing with optional static types is superior to static typing with bolted-on dynamism - the latter is 'premature optimization'
The minimum feature set for a modern language includes closures, iterators, list comprehensions, first-class parser support, and cross-platform GUI
Performance comparable to Java is essential, which requires clever solutions around eval() and other dynamic features
money_quotes: If you want to spare yourself a lot of angst in deciding which programming language to use, then I recommend this simple rule: Go ugly early.
Having a deep understanding of compilers is what separates the wheat from the chaff.
We have yet to see a language that is (a) popular and (b) doesn't suck. Do you disagree? If so, I don't mind. It just means your standards aren't as high as mine.
Adding in optional static types is the ideal solution... static + optional dynamic features is premature optimization, plain and simple: the root of all evil.
One of the biggest reasons people haven't adopted Ruby or Python is the lack of IDE support.
Ruby doesn't actually need automated refactoring tools in the way Java does; it's like refusing to switch to an electric car because there's no place to put the gasoline.
The features I've outlined are basically the minimal set of requirements for not sucking.
Nobody ever accuses programmers of being wise.
key_insight: Language adoption is determined not by elegance or technical superiority, but by meeting a high bar of practical requirements including familiar syntax, corporate backing, strong tooling, and comprehensive features - optimizing for programmer expectations rather than ideals.
